text: Laparoscopic splenectomy anatomy is that laparoscopy generally the whenever you perform the massive splenectomy should be avoided and normally unless the spleen is double in the size it will be hidden into the rib cage so it has five ligament that is pancreas splenic ligament gastro splenic ligament phrenicoesplenic ligament, esplenocolic ligament, and phrenicocolic ligament. These all are the five ligaments which is important to keep in mind whenever you do laparoscopic esplenectomy. Case selection is important. Normal indications are idiopathic thrombocytopenic purpura, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, microspherocytosis, benign tumors of the cyst, and HIV-related thrombocytopenia. There are certain contraindications also. Relative contraindication is hematological malignancies and moderate splenomegaly. And the absolute contraindication is massive splenomegaly or if the patient has the portal hypertension, In those situations, it should be avoided. Patient position. Patient should be semi-lateral position. You will stand in front of the patient. One assistant will be behind the patient. Nurse should be on the right side next to the surgeon to give the instrument. And monitor, either you can one on the left side or you can take two also so that you and your assistant both can see. You have to put one cushion so that the costal margin will be little go up. And this is the usual position of the patient. That is semi-lateral position. The monitor will be behind and this will be the position of the patient.

The surgeon positions the laparoscopic ports approximately five to ten centimeters above the umbilicus on the midclavicular line to avoid obstruction by the bowel and ensure visibility of the splenic hilum. A small incision is made, and a Veress needle is inserted perpendicularly into the abdominal wall to create pneumoperitoneum. After performing suction irrigation and the hanging drop test, a trocar is placed at the same level, followed by the introduction of the telescope. The arc of the telescope's view is adjusted based on the size of the spleen.

So this is the baseball diamond concept. Your target is the hilum. And this is the hilum here. So this will make a shape of a Baseball field or diamond. Then you will start your surgery. Yes. Port is more or less like nephrectomy. But in nephrectomy, this port will be little down and this port will be little up. Because your toilet is shifting down. Yes. Vertically in the same line. And that also depends upon the size of kidney. Exactly same way as here it depends upon the size of the spleen. Now once you have entered, pneumo-peritoneum also has to be made in the same way. But it should not go vertical otherwise we can damage the bobble. So it should be perpendicular to the abdominal wall. And you will decide the position after putting the telescope. Because it may vary according to the size of the spleen.

The surgeon positions the ports for the splenectomy, with the telescope placed along the midclavicular line, and additional ports on the mid-axillary and posterior axillary lines. An optional epigastric port is used for a liver retractor. The procedure begins with the dissection of the splenocolic or phrenicocolic ligament, followed by the short gastric vessels using bipolar, ligature, harmonic, or endo-GI linear staplers. The surgeon then approaches the hilum, retracting the kidney slightly upward to separate the splenocolic ligament.

The surgeon uses a hook, tripolar, or harmonic device to carefully dissect the inferior pole of the spleen, ensuring proximity to the spleen to avoid injury to surrounding structures. This dissection frees the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments, allowing the spleen's lower pole to be lifted, enhancing visibility of the short gastric vessels and the hilum.

The surgeon applies a titanium clip to control bleeding during the dissection of the short gastric vessels. A 12 mm port is enlarged to introduce a vascular stapler, specifically the auto suture endo-GIA-II, to secure the vessels at the hilum. The stapler is fired, deploying three rows of staples on either side of the vessel, with slight oozing visible along the stapler line.

The surgeon uses the auto-suture Endo-GIA-II stapler to separate the splenic vessels, requiring two cartridges for complete separation. The stapler is fired, and the blade moves to cut the tissue, indicated by a green signal showing appropriate tissue thickness. The phrenico-splenic ligament is detached using a hook. The spleen is placed inside a commercially available D-size endo bag with a metal ring, which is self-retaining. The bag is introduced into the surgical field, and the spleen is negotiated into it. A purse string is applied to secure the spleen inside the bag before extraction.

The surgeon removes the spleen by manually morselating it into small pieces using ovum forceps, ensuring the endo bag remains intact to prevent contamination and splenosis. A nasogastric tube is placed in the splenic fossa as a drain for any fluid collection. The wound is closed with stitches and cyanoacrylate glue, followed by plaster adhesive application.

The surgeon uses a stapler with a Gore-Tex sheath to complete the separation of the spleen. The cut part of the spleen is transferred into an endo bag for manual morselation. The surgical field is irrigated and suctioned, with minimal bleeding observed. A hematonic matrix, such as flow seal, is applied over the staple line to control oozing.

It is a unique combination of the patented gelatin granules and human thrombin. So provide the fast effective hemostatic. So this small granules of the gelatin and with the human thrombin will make a matrix network and that will keep the complete bleeding separated. And now you can put some irrigation and automatically it will be washed out. Excess of the granules will be separated. And this way you can achieve the hemostasis.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device, which combines bipolar electrosurgery with a cutting blade, to dissect the splenocolic and phrenicocolic ligaments. The device is employed for both blunt and sharp dissection, while ensuring the separation of the artery and vein to prevent arteriovenous fistula. The short gastric vessels are coagulated and cut, with care taken to avoid the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection using a placelet, following the French technique of lateral mobilization of the spleen. The spleen is flipped over the stomach before proceeding to the hilum, without skeletonizing or securing the artery initially.

The surgeon detaches remaining fibers of the splenocolic ligament after freeing the inferior pole, using a retractor to lift the spleen and a grasper to keep the bowel away. The Maryland dissector is used to lift the spleen with its convexity towards the spleen to avoid bleeding. The spleen is now completely free from the colon, revealing the tail of the pancreas. The hilum is approached, with careful dissection around the perihilar fat, vein, and artery, creating a window between them. An accessory spleen is also checked for.

The surgeon lifts the inferior pole of the spleen and holds the artery with a Maryland dissector, creating a window around the artery. Energy is used to seal the artery, with intermittent activation to shrink the vessel lumen and seal it with denatured protein. The blade cuts the artery, and the vein is addressed afterward, allowing collected blood to return and collapse the vein. The major vessels are secured, and the remaining part of the surgery proceeds without bleeding.

The surgeon uses a tripolar device to tackle remaining short gastric vessels, visible near the stomach. The frenico-esplenic ligament is separated, completing the dissection. The video then demonstrates a lateral approach from Éricard, France, with the patient in lateral position and triangulation maintained using ports. Adhesions are cleared with a hook, and the spleen is mobilized laterally without initially addressing short gastric vessels. A peanut sponge is used for blunt dissection and pressure application on oozing areas.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the spleen, which is flipped and positioned against the left lobe of the liver. The gastro-hepatic ligament is separated, and the hilum is exposed with visible pulsation of the artery. The perihilar fats are separated, and a window is created between the artery and vein. A vascular stapler is used to secure the artery, followed by the vein. The remaining short gastric vessels are secured using a ligature. The spleen is placed in an endo bag for removal, with the dorsal aspect touching the gallbladder.
